annotations.label: Stationary point (one variable)

anchor.snippet: a stationary point of a differentiable function of one variable is a point on the graph of the function where the function's derivative is zero

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named stationary/critical-point definition, but the condition is directly expressible as `deriv f x = 0` and underlies Fermat's theorem.

annotations.label: Stationary point (several variables)

anchor.snippet: a stationary point is a point on the surface of the graph where all its partial derivatives are zero

annotations.note: No named multivariate stationary-point definition exists, but it is expressible as `fderiv ℝ f x = 0`, the hypothesis form used in `IsLocalExtr.fderiv_eq_zero`.

annotations.label: Geometric interpretation (horizontal tangent)

anchor.snippet: they correspond to the points on the graph where the tangent is horizontal

annotations.note: This is an informal geometric restatement (horizontal tangent) with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Turning point

anchor.snippet: A turning point of a differentiable function is a point at which the derivative has an isolated zero and changes sign at the point

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of turning point (isolated sign-changing zero of the derivative).

annotations.label: Turning points are stationary points

anchor.snippet: A turning point is thus a stationary point, but not all stationary points are turning points

annotations.note: Without a turning-point notion, only the underlying content (a local extremum is stationary, via Fermat's theorem) is present as `IsLocalExtr.deriv_eq_zero`.

annotations.label: Non-turning stationary points are horizontal inflection points

anchor.snippet: If the function is twice differentiable, the isolated stationary points that are not turning points are horizontal inflection points

annotations.note: Mathlib defines neither inflection points nor turning points, so this classification statement is absent.

annotations.label: Inflection point that is not a turning point

anchor.snippet: which is also an inflection point, but is not a turning point

annotations.note: No inflection-point or turning-point machinery exists in Mathlib to express this example.

annotations.label: Local minimum

anchor.snippet: a local minimum ( minimal turning point or relative minimum ) is one where the derivative of the function changes from negative to positive

annotations.note: Local minimum is `IsLocalMin`; the derivative-sign-change characterization is the first-derivative test `isLocalMin_of_deriv`.

annotations.label: Local maximum

anchor.snippet: a local maximum ( maximal turning point or relative maximum ) is one where the derivative of the function changes from positive to negative

annotations.note: Local maximum is `IsLocalMax`; the derivative-sign-change characterization is the first-derivative test `isLocalMax_of_deriv`.

annotations.label: Rising point of inflection

anchor.snippet: a rising point of inflection (or inflexion ) is one where the derivative of the function is positive on both sides of the stationary point

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of (rising) point of inflection.

annotations.label: Falling point of inflection

anchor.snippet: a falling point of inflection (or inflexion ) is one where the derivative of the function is negative on both sides of the stationary point

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of (falling) point of inflection.

annotations.label: Local extrema

anchor.snippet: The first two options are collectively known as

annotations.note: Local extremum (local min or max) is `IsLocalExtr`, defined as `IsLocalMin ∨ IsLocalMax`.

annotations.label: Global extremum

anchor.snippet: is called a global (or absolute) extremum

annotations.note: A global extremum over the whole domain is `IsExtrOn f univ a` (with `IsMaxOn`/`IsMinOn`), the set-relative form generalizing the global case.

annotations.label: Saddle points

anchor.snippet: The last two options—stationary points that are not local extrema—are known as saddle points

annotations.note: Mathlib's `IsSaddlePointOn` is the minimax (min-in-one-argument, max-in-another) notion, not the calculus sense of a stationary point that is not a local extremum.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem on extrema

anchor.snippet: By Fermat's theorem , global extrema must occur

annotations.note: Fermat's theorem (the derivative vanishes at a local extremum) is `IsLocalExtr.deriv_eq_zero`, with `IsLocalExtr.hasFDerivAt_eq_zero` for the multivariate form.

annotations.label: Second derivative test

anchor.snippet: can in some cases be determined by examining the second derivative

annotations.note: The second-derivative test is `isLocalMin_of_deriv_deriv_pos` (and `isLocalMax_of_deriv_deriv_neg`).

annotations.label: Determining nature by function values

anchor.snippet: A more straightforward way of determining the nature of a stationary point is by examining the function values between the stationary points

annotations.note: This is an informal curve-sketching heuristic with no direct Mathlib declaration, though monotonicity-from-derivative lemmas underlie it.

annotations.label: Inflection point of x^3

anchor.snippet: A simple example of a point of inflection is the function

annotations.note: No inflection-point concept exists in Mathlib to support this example.

annotations.label: Stationary point via directional derivatives

anchor.snippet: are those points x 0 where the derivative in every direction equals zero

annotations.note: Directional derivatives are formalized as `lineDeriv`, but Mathlib has no definition of a stationary point as a point where every directional derivative vanishes.

annotations.label: x^4: not an inflection point

anchor.snippet: this point is not a point of inflection

annotations.note: Mathlib has no inflection-point notion to express this example about x^4.

annotations.label: sin(x): inflection, not stationary

anchor.snippet: But this is not a stationary point, rather it is a point of inflection

annotations.note: No inflection-point machinery exists, so this sin(x) example is not formalized.

annotations.label: x^3: stationary and inflection

anchor.snippet: This is both a stationary point and a point of inflection

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks inflection-point definitions, so this combined x^3 example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Zero function: non-isolated stationary point

anchor.snippet: The point 0 is a non-isolated stationary point which is not a turning point nor a horizontal point of inflection

annotations.note: Without stationary/turning/inflection-point notions, this classification example for the zero function is not formalized.

annotations.label: x^5 sin(1/x): non-isolated stationary point

anchor.snippet: 0 is a stationary point that is not isolated

annotations.note: Mathlib has no stationary-point notion to express this non-isolated example for x^5 sin(1/x).
